title: Modular Enterprise AI Stacks Are Being Positioned Against Single-Stack Dependence
intent: Show that modular AI architecture is emerging as an explicit enterprise design principle, not just an engineering preference.

source.title: The Linux Foundation Annual Report 2025
source.methodology: Annual report describing Linux Foundation ecosystem initiatives including OPEA.

fact.primary_stat: The Linux Foundation’s 2025 annual report said OPEA enables organizations to mix and match compatible components rather than being locked into any single vendor’s stack.
supporting_stats: The report describes OPEA as providing standardized, composable building blocks for enterprise AI systems.
It frames interoperability and modularity as the same principles that made Linux and Kubernetes successful, now applied to AI.
The report also argues that smaller specialized models matter where latency, cost, and local deployment are important.

business_relevance: Enterprise AI architecture is shifting toward composable building blocks and away from single-stack dependence.
Model portability and replaceable components are now part of the cost and resilience conversation.
dzmitryi_commentary: This is a strong match for my side-by-side viewpoint. In SAP and AMS settings, the goal is not to rebuild ERP outside SAP; it is to compose small services around it: retrieval, summarization, routing, diagnostics, and agent control. That gives the business room to swap models, vendors, and orchestration layers without redoing the full operating model.

provenance.note: Extracted and curated by Dzmitryi Kharlanau; enriched for attribution and crawler indexing.

meta.summary: Show that modular AI architecture is emerging as an explicit enterprise design principle, not just an engineering preference.
